region_of_origin: Widely cultivated; commonly used in Singapore and global cuisines

ontology.foodex2_description: Mushrooms and fungi — cultivated mushrooms (Agaricus bisporus), boiled
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT code 256319004 refers to the Agaricus bisporus species substance; no specific boiled-preparation SNOMED CT code exists. closest_parent used as match type.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of boiled button mushrooms is approximately 80 g, equivalent to about 5–6 medium mushroom caps as a side dish or ingredient in a cooked meal.

nutrient_source.title: Boiled Button Mushroom — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database for boiled button mushroom (F14030001). All core macros and micronutrients reflect the HPB canonical entry at confidence 0.95. Fatty acid fractions and selected micronutrients supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169251) where HPB does not report those fields.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; mushroom GI reference
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value for boiled button mushroom exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on cooked mushrooms (Agaricus bisporus) reported in peer-reviewed GI literature (Atkinson et al., 2021, AJCN). Glycemic load per 80 g serving = (15 × 5.29 × 0.80) / 100 = 0.6, classified as low.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (2 mg/100 g) and high potassium (356 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Boiled button mushrooms are highly suitable for hypertensive individuals; their negligible sodium content (2 mg/100 g) and high potassium (356 mg/100 g) provide a potassium-to-sodium ratio that actively supports blood pressure reduction in line with DASH dietary principles.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~15), low carbohydrate load (5.3 g/100 g), and moderate dietary fibre (2.2 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Boiled button mushrooms are appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; their low GI (~15), low net carbohydrate content, and dietary fibre contribute to attenuated postprandial glucose response and improved glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.06 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, high potassium, and beta-glucan fibre
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Boiled button mushrooms are cardioprotective; their negligible saturated fat, zero cholesterol, high potassium, and beta-glucan dietary fibre support lipid management and vascular health, making them suitable for cardiovascular disease prevention diets.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beta-glucan content, iron (1.74 mg/100 g), negligible saturated fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Button mushrooms provide beta-glucans with immune-modulating properties and iron (1.74 mg/100 g) with negligible saturated fat, meeting positive anti-inflammatory dietary criteria aligned with EULAR dietary guidance for rheumatoid arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Boiled button mushroom (Agaricus bisporus) is a minimally processed vegetable-category food prepared by simmering fresh white button mushrooms in water until tender. The result is pale white caps and stems with a soft, yielding texture and a mild, earthy umami flavour. Button mushrooms are among the most widely cultivated and consumed fungi globally and are a staple ingredient in Singaporean, Western, and pan-Asian cuisines. Per 100 g, boiled button mushrooms provide only 28 kcal, making them an exceptionally low-calorie food. They supply 2.17 g protein, 0.47 g fat (of which only 0.06 g is saturated), and 5.29 g carbohydrate with 2.2 g dietary fibre and 2.34 g sugar. Sodium is negligible at 2 mg/100 g while potassium is notably high at 356 mg/100 g, yielding an outstanding potassium-to-sodium ratio beneficial for blood pressure management. Iron content is 1.74 mg/100 g, contributing to daily iron requirements. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 15 (low), making them suitable for blood glucose management. Clinically, boiled button mushrooms are appropriate for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease. They are naturally vegan, vegetarian, gluten-free, halal, and kosher, with no known allergens. Beta-glucan polysaccharides in the cell walls confer immune-modulating properties relevant to inflammatory conditions.
common_user_queries: Are boiled mushrooms good for diabetics?
How many calories in boiled button mushrooms?
Can I eat mushrooms if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of button mushrooms?
Nutritional value of cooked white mushrooms
Are mushrooms good for heart health?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F14030001) at confidence 0.95. The food is an exact match in the primary regional approved source. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2021) as no direct GI entry exists for this preparation; this reduces GI confidence to medium but does not affect overall record confidence given the strong macro/micro provenance.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (0.06 g), monounsaturated fat (0.01 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.19 g), and trans_fat_g (0.0 g) values are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for mushrooms, white, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt (FDC ID 169251) as HPB does not report fatty acid fractions; this is documented as an adapted supplementary source. 2. Vitamin C (2.1 mg), B-vitamins, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water content are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169251) as supplementary micronutrient data not fully reported by HPB. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is set to -1: retinol activity equivalent data for boiled button mushroom are not reported in HPB Foodo (F14030001) or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169251) for this exact preparation; mushrooms contain negligible provitamin A carotenoids and RAE values are not consistently published for this food. 4. vitamin_e_mg is set to -1: tocopherol data for boiled button mushroom are not reported in HPB Foodo (F14030001) or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169251) for this exact preparation; detailed vitamin E fractions are absent from both approved sources consulted. 5. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on cooked Agaricus bisporus per Atkinson et al. (2021) International Tables of GI and GL Values, AJCN; no direct boiled button mushroom GI entry exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 6. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB and USDA FoodData Central; mushrooms are fungi and contain no dietary cholesterol. 7. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero; mushrooms are a plant-kingdom food and do not naturally contain vitamin B12. 8. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero; no industrial hydrogenation involved in boiling mushrooms. 9. Ontology: FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03301710 is the closest defensible match for button mushroom food product; FoodEx2 code A00LC represents cultivated mushrooms. SNOMED CT 256319004 is a broader species-level substance code with match type set to closest_parent; no specific boiled-preparation SNOMED CT code exists.
